<!-- Auto-generated by BioNexus brain-appearance research workflow. Cited, adversarially reviewed. -->

# How the brain should look — BioNexus

This reference document defines the target appearance of the 3D human brain in the BioNexus anatomy viewer. It answers two questions: (1) what a real human brain actually *looks like* — its form, surface texture, subdivisions, spatial relationships, and true living color — and (2) how the leading 3D anatomy platforms render that brain in practice, including the conventions BioNexus should follow. A final **visual acceptance checklist** distills both into concrete, checkable criteria a reviewer can use to confirm that a rendered brain "looks right" rather than reading as a smooth egg, peach, or featureless blob.

---

## Visual anatomy of the human brain

This section describes what the brain *looks like* — its gross external form, surface texture, subdivisions, spatial relationships, and true living color — as a reference for modelling and labelling a 3D educational anatomy viewer. Figures are approximate adult averages; individual brains vary.

### Overall form, size, and orientation

Viewed from above, the whole brain has a rounded, **ovoid ("egg-shaped") outline that is often likened to a walnut** because of its deeply wrinkled surface. It is **broader posteriorly than anteriorly** — the **parietal and temporal regions are the widest** part, tapering to the narrower frontal pole in front (and to a pointed occipital pole behind) — giving the top-down silhouette its egg-like asymmetry (blunt/wide behind, narrower in front).

Typical adult dimensions and mass:

| Property | Approximate value |
|---|---|
| Mass (adult) | ~1,300–1,400 g (≈2% of body weight) |
| Length (front–back) | ~16–17 cm |
| Width (side–side) | ~14 cm |
| Height (top–bottom) | ~9–10 cm |
| Volume | ~1,100–1,300 mL |

Male brains average slightly larger (~1,345 g) than female (~1,222 g), with roughly 1 cm less in each linear dimension in females ([Physics Factbook](https://hypertextbook.com/facts/2003/RachelScottRosenbluth.shtml); [Wikipedia — Brain size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain_size)).

**Standard anatomical orientation for the viewer:** *anterior* = front (frontal pole), *posterior* = back (occipital pole), *superior* = top (vertex), *inferior* = base. The brain is not symmetric front-to-back — the occipital region overhangs the cerebellum posteriorly.

### The two cerebral hemispheres

The **cerebrum** is by far the largest component, forming roughly 80–87% of total brain volume and sitting **superior and anterior** to everything else ([StatPearls — Cerebral Hemisphere](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549789/); [TeachMeAnatomy — Cerebrum](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neuroanatomy/structures/cerebrum/)). It is split down the midline into a **left** and a **right cerebral hemisphere** by a deep midsagittal cleft, the **longitudinal fissure** (median longitudinal fissure). A vertical sheet of dura mater, the **falx cerebri**, descends into this fissure between the two hemispheres.

The hemispheres are not fully separated: at the floor of the longitudinal fissure they are joined by the **corpus callosum**, the largest white-matter commissure in the brain — about **10 cm long and containing an estimated 200–300 million axons** that carry information between the two sides ([Wikipedia — Corpus callosum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corpus_callosum); [Kenhub — Corpus callosum](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/corpus-callosum)). In a 3D model the two hemispheres therefore read as mirror-image halves bridged internally by a broad white band deep in the central cleft.

### Cortical surface texture: gyri, sulci, and the four lobes

The outer surface of each hemisphere — the **cerebral cortex** — is intensely folded. This folding is the brain's most recognizable visual feature:

- **Gyri** (singular *gyrus*) are the **raised ridges** — the rounded crests of each convolution.
- **Sulci** (singular *sulcus*) are the **grooves** between gyri; the deepest and most constant grooves are sometimes called **fissures**.

This convoluted packing greatly increases cortical surface area within the fixed volume of the skull ([NCBI — Anatomy of the Cerebral Cortex](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK575742/); [Kenhub — Cerebral cortex](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebral-cortex)).

Two landmark sulci are the primary lobe boundaries and should be modelled as consistent, deep, reproducible landmarks:

- **Central sulcus** (fissure of Rolando): runs obliquely down the lateral surface and separates the **frontal lobe (in front)** from the **parietal lobe (behind)**. The gyrus immediately anterior to it (precentral gyrus) is the primary motor strip; the gyrus immediately posterior (postcentral gyrus) is the primary somatosensory strip.
- **Lateral sulcus** (Sylvian fissure): a deep horizontal cleft on the side of the brain that separates the **temporal lobe (below)** from the frontal and parietal lobes (above) ([Britannica — Lobes of the cerebral cortex](https://www.britannica.com/science/human-nervous-system/Lobes-of-the-cerebral-cortex); [TeachMeAnatomy — Cerebrum](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neuroanatomy/structures/cerebrum/)).

Each hemisphere is conventionally divided into **four lobes**, named for the overlying skull bones — a helpful mnemonic for placement in the viewer:

| Lobe | Position | Key surface bounds |
|---|---|---|
| **Frontal** | Anterior (front) | Anterior to central sulcus; above lateral sulcus |
| **Parietal** | Superior-posterior (top/back) | Behind central sulcus; above lateral sulcus |
| **Temporal** | Inferior-lateral (side, below) | Below the lateral sulcus, under the temporal bone |
| **Occipital** | Posterior-most (very back) | Rearmost tip; rests above the cerebellum on the tentorium |

(A fifth region, the **insula**, lies hidden deep within the lateral sulcus and is not visible on the intact surface.)

### The cerebellum

The **cerebellum** ("little brain") sits **posterior and inferior** to the cerebrum, tucked into the **posterior cranial fossa, beneath (inferior to) the occipital lobes**. It is separated from the overlying occipital lobes by a tent-like fold of dura mater, the **tentorium cerebelli** ([Kenhub — Cerebellum gross anatomy](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebellum-gross-anatomy)). It lies **dorsal to (behind) the pons and medulla**, from which it is separated by the **fourth ventricle**.

Visually the cerebellum is easy to distinguish from the cerebrum:

- It is much **smaller** — roughly **10% of total brain volume/weight** — yet, remarkably, it contains **more than half of all the neurons in the entire brain** because its cortex is so densely packed.
- Its surface is not thrown into broad gyri but into many **fine, thin, closely spaced parallel ridges called folia** ("leaves"). Compared with cerebral gyri, cerebellar folia are **smaller, more numerous, more uniform, and run in parallel horizontal bands**, giving a finely lined or "pleated" appearance ([Kenhub — Cerebellum gross anatomy](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebellum-gross-anatomy)).
- Structurally it comprises two **cerebellar hemispheres** joined in the midline by a narrow, worm-like ridge, the **vermis**.

In a cut section, the branching white-matter core of the cerebellum forms the classic tree-like pattern known as the **arbor vitae** ("tree of life").

### The brainstem

The **brainstem** is the stalk that connects the cerebrum and cerebellum above to the spinal cord below. It occupies a **central-inferior** position, emerging from the underside of the cerebrum and running downward roughly along the midline, largely hidden from a top or side view by the overhanging cerebral hemispheres. From superior to inferior it has three parts ([TeachMeAnatomy — Brainstem](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neuroanatomy/brainstem/); [Wikipedia — Brainstem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brainstem)):

1. **Midbrain (mesencephalon)** — the uppermost segment, continuous above with the **diencephalon** (thalamus and hypothalamus), which in turn connects to the cerebral hemispheres. *(The diencephalon is a distinct forebrain division, not part of the cerebrum/telencephalon proper.)*
2. **Pons** — the bulging middle segment; the cerebellum attaches to its posterior aspect.
3. **Medulla oblongata** — the tapering lowest segment.

The medulla passes through the **foramen magnum** (the large opening at the base of the skull) and **becomes continuous with the spinal cord** below — there is no sharp external boundary; the brainstem simply narrows and merges into the cord ([Britannica — Medulla oblongata](https://www.britannica.com/science/medulla-oblongata); [Cleveland Clinic — Brainstem](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/21598-brainstem)). For the viewer, model the brainstem as a downward-projecting stem behind the cerebrum and in front of the cerebellum, tapering smoothly into the spinal cord.

### Color of a living / fresh brain

An important correction for realistic rendering: the familiar uniform **grey** of specimens is an artifact of preservation. Formalin (formaldehyde) fixation leaches blood from the tissue and hardens it, dulling the color. A **living or fresh brain is quite different** ([ScienceInsights](https://scienceinsights.org/what-color-is-the-brain-its-not-just-gray-matter/); [Biology Insights](https://biologyinsights.com/are-brains-pink-the-real-color-of-a-living-human-brain/)):

- The overall surface is a warm **pinkish-grey to beige/tan**, tending toward **pinkish-red** in life because the cortex is extremely vascular and full of oxygenated blood.
- Running across the surface, within the delicate innermost meningeal layer (the **pia mater**), is a visible tracery of **pial blood vessels**: fine **arteries** carry bright scarlet oxygenated blood, while **veins** appear darker, deep red to purplish. Modelling these superficial vessels adds strong realism.

Two tissue types account for the internal color contrast:

- **Grey matter** — neuronal cell bodies, dendrites, and unmyelinated fibres — forms the thin **outer cortical layer** of the cerebrum and cerebellum plus deep nuclei (e.g. basal ganglia). In life it is a **pinkish-grey / tan**, not flat grey, owing to dense capillaries.
- **White matter** — myelinated axon bundles — lies **beneath the cortex**, forming the bulk of the interior. Its pale, **pinkish-white** appearance comes from the fatty (lipid) **myelin** sheaths ([Wikipedia — White matter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_matter); [Wikipedia — Grey matter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grey_matter)).

So in cross-section the cerebrum shows a **thin grey rind over a larger white-matter core** (with islands of deep grey nuclei) — a key visual cue if the model exposes a cut plane.

### Spatial relationships at a glance

For placing components correctly in 3D space:

- **Cerebrum** — largest; occupies the **top, front, sides, and back**, overhanging the structures below.
- **Diencephalon** (thalamus/hypothalamus) — a small forebrain division sitting **centrally between the cerebral hemispheres and the midbrain**, largely hidden from external view (usually seen only on a midsagittal cut).
- **Cerebellum** — **posterior and inferior**, beneath the occipital lobes (separated by the tentorium cerebelli), behind the brainstem.
- **Brainstem** — **central and inferior**, projecting downward along the midline between the cerebrum and cerebellum, continuing through the foramen magnum into the **spinal cord**.

**Sources**

- [Gray's Anatomy of the Human Body (Bartleby edition) — The Brain / Encephalon](https://www.bartleby.com/lit-hub/anatomy-of-the-human-body/) (classic reference for gross morphology, hemispheres, lobes, and brainstem subdivisions)
- Kenhub — [Cerebellum: gross anatomy](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebellum-gross-anatomy), [Cerebral cortex](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebral-cortex), [Corpus callosum](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/corpus-callosum), [Brainstem](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/the-brainstem)
- TeachMeAnatomy — [The Cerebrum](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neuroanatomy/structures/cerebrum/), [Brainstem Anatomy](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neuroanatomy/brainstem/)
- Wikipedia — [Corpus callosum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corpus_callosum), [Brainstem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brainstem), [Brain size](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain_size), [White matter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_matter), [Grey matter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grey_matter)
- NCBI Bookshelf / StatPearls — [Anatomy of the Cerebral Cortex](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK575742/), [Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Hemisphere](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549789/)
- Britannica — [Lobes of the cerebral cortex](https://www.britannica.com/science/human-nervous-system/Lobes-of-the-cerebral-cortex), [Medulla oblongata](https://www.britannica.com/science/medulla-oblongata); Cleveland Clinic — [Brainstem](https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/21598-brainstem)
- Living-brain color: [ScienceInsights](https://scienceinsights.org/what-color-is-the-brain-its-not-just-gray-matter/), [Biology Insights](https://biologyinsights.com/are-brains-pink-the-real-color-of-a-living-human-brain/)

---

## How 3D anatomy apps render the brain

A survey of the leading platforms — BioDigital Human, Complete Anatomy (3D4Medical/Elsevier), Visible Body, Kenhub, Zygote, and high-scoring Sketchfab anatomy models — shows a fairly consistent set of conventions, with the main split being *naturalistic* rendering (a realistic wet, pinkish brain) versus *didactic* rendering (flat color-coded regions for teaching).

### 1. Color palette

The default naturalistic palette is a warm **grey-pink / beige** tissue color. A fresh brain is a pinkish-grey organ: grey matter (cortex) reads as a muted grey-pink, while exposed white matter and the brainstem read as a creamy off-white to beige. True-color renderings sampled directly from cadaver cryosection data confirm that neural tissue sits in this low-saturation pink-to-tan range, with "minimal color variations from the living body for most tissues" ([PMC true-color study](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9296043/)).

Alongside this, every major platform offers a **didactic color-coding mode** where each region gets a distinct flat hue for identification. BioDigital's Paint tool ships a default palette of 10 ready-made colors specifically so users can "distinguish multiple structures from one another (for example, the lobes of the brain)" ([BioDigital Paint](https://support.biodigital.com/hc/en-us/articles/360024206493-Paint-a-model)). MRI-derived Sketchfab teaching models follow the same pattern — segmented in 3D Slicer, then given per-region colors in Blender ([Colour Coded Whole Brain, Anat2022](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/colour-coded-whole-brain-gyri-and-sulci-988681f752fa484190ecc7cef39fff90)).

### 2. Gyri/sulci: real geometry vs. faked, and polygon budgets

The folding pattern is handled two ways depending on the target:

- **Real folded mesh** in high-fidelity reference and desktop models. Zygote's brain carries the sulci and gyri as named, actual geometry (central sulcus, lateral sulcus, precentral/postcentral gyri, etc.) at roughly **436,800 triangles** ([Zygote 3D Brain](https://www.zygote.com/poly-models/3d-brain/3d-human-brain)). MRI-segmentation models inherit true folds directly from the scan — e.g. a **713.9k-triangle** whole brain from a segmented T1 MRI ([Anat2022](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/colour-coded-whole-brain-gyri-and-sulci-988681f752fa484190ecc7cef39fff90)) — and ZBrush-sculpted assets land around **389k triangles / 194k vertices** with the folds sculpted in ([Human Brain Cross Section, zames1992](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/human-brain-cross-section-anatomy-74436611a92446cab071b0d982534d37)).
- **Baked into normal + ambient-occlusion maps** on real-time/web-optimized models, where the silhouette folds stay as geometry but the fine relief is faked. A representative real-time asset is a **~40k-polygon (~98k-triangle), subdivision-ready base mesh** carrying the deep-fold detail in 8K normal/AO/roughness maps rather than raw polys ([3D Brain Anatomy, 3D4SCI](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/3d-brain-anatomy-36870e0970f044a8957b0af3a180a7eb)).

So typical budgets are roughly **30k–100k triangles for web/mobile real-time** (folds mostly in normal maps) versus **~400k–700k+ triangles for high-fidelity reference** (folds as true mesh). Visible Body's models likewise rely on "high resolution image textures and normal maps based on non-overlapping UVs" to carry surface detail on lighter meshes.

### 3. Distinguishing cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem

The primary cue is **shape/geometry**, which the folded mesh already conveys: the cerebrum as large, deeply folded hemispheres; the cerebellum as a tightly packed, finely foliated ("tree-like" folia) mass sitting postero-inferiorly; and the brainstem as a smooth central stalk. This matches how atlases describe them — the cerebellum "lies posterior to the brainstem... separated from the cerebrum by a fold of dura (the tentorium cerebelli)," and the brainstem is the smooth region of midbrain, pons, and medulla ([Kenhub: cerebellum & brainstem](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebellum-and-brainstem), [Kenhub: cerebrum](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cerebrum)).

On top of form, apps add **segmentation into separate selectable materials** so a color-code can be toggled on. Visible Body explicitly splits the brain into four regions — cerebrum, diencephalon, cerebellum, brainstem ([Visible Body: The Brain](https://www.visiblebody.com/learn/nervous/brain)) — and 3B-style and Sketchfab color-coded models assign each a distinct hue. In naturalistic mode the three share one palette and are read by shape and subtle tonal shifts; in didactic mode each gets its own flat color.

### 4. Meninges (dura / arachnoid / pia)

The strong convention is to model the meninges as **separate, individually toggleable covering layers** rather than baking them in, and to make the inner layers **translucent**. Zygote ships dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater as distinct grouped layers around the brain ([Zygote](https://www.zygote.com/poly-models/3d-brain/3d-human-brain)); Visible Body's nervous-system models "include cerebral meninges and dura of the spinal cord"; and Complete Anatomy added a dedicated detailed **Scalp & Meninges** model distinguishing the tough opaque dura, the "web-like" arachnoid, and the "delicate vascular" pia ([3D4Medical scalp & meninges](https://3d4medical.com/blog/scalp-and-meninges)). Rendered, the dura reads as a semi-opaque outer sheath and the arachnoid/pia as thin translucent films over the cortex.

However, the **default brain view almost always omits the meninges** to expose the bare cortex, offering them only as an optional layer — and many lightweight web/Sketchfab educational models omit them entirely.

### 5. Lighting and material conventions

All the modern platforms use a **physically-based (PBR)** workflow — base color + metallic/roughness + normal + AO maps ([zames1992](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/human-brain-cross-section-anatomy-74436611a92446cab071b0d982534d37), [3D4SCI](https://sketchfab.com/3d-models/3d-brain-anatomy-36870e0970f044a8957b0af3a180a7eb)). The brain is rendered as a **slightly glossy, moist "wet" surface, not flat matte and not mirror**: low-to-moderate roughness gives a soft specular sheen consistent with living tissue, and a subtle **subsurface / translucent** quality communicates soft, semi-translucent tissue. Complete Anatomy in particular is built around "high-fidelity," lifelike real-time rendering (now on Apple's Metal engine) that leans on this moist, subsurface look ([Complete Anatomy / 3D4Medical](https://3d4medical.com/), [Elsevier](https://www.elsevier.com/products/complete-anatomy)). Deep **ambient occlusion baked into the sulci** is doing much of the visual work — it darkens the folds so the gyral pattern reads even under soft lighting. Environment/IBL (HDR) lighting plus a key light and gentle rim light keep the folds legible without harsh flattening.

### Best-practice rendering conventions for a web-based educational viewer

- **Base color:** default to a warm, low-saturation **grey-pink / beige** cortex; keep white matter and brainstem a shade lighter/creamier.
- **Didactic toggle:** offer an optional flat **color-code per region** (cerebrum / cerebellum / brainstem, and lobes) with a small, consistent palette (BioDigital-style ~8–10 colors).
- **Geometry:** keep the **silhouette folds as real mesh** but budget for web (~50k–150k triangles); push fine gyral relief into **normal + AO maps** on a subdivision-ready base rather than raw polygons.
- **Region cues:** rely first on **shape**, and provide selectable, separately-materialed structures so users can isolate/recolor cerebrum, cerebellum, and brainstem.
- **Meninges:** model dura/arachnoid/pia as **separate, toggleable layers** — dura semi-opaque, arachnoid/pia translucent — but ship them **OFF by default** so the cortex is visible.
- **Material:** PBR with **low-to-moderate roughness** for a moist sheen, a touch of **subsurface/translucency**, and **strong baked AO in the sulci**; avoid dead-flat matte and avoid mirror-gloss.
- **Lighting:** soft **HDR/IBL environment + key light + gentle rim/fresnel** so folds read in depth; never a single harsh light that flattens the sulci.
- **Performance:** compress meshes (e.g. Draco/meshopt), use 2K–4K textures with LODs, and prefer normal-map fold detail over high polygon counts for smooth web/mobile interaction.

### Sources

- BioDigital Human — [platform](https://www.biodigital.com/) · [Paint tool / default palette](https://support.biodigital.com/hc/en-us/articles/360024206493-Paint-a-model)

- Visible Body — [The Human Brain](https://www.visiblebody.com/learn/nervous/brain) · [Virtual brain dissection](https://www.visiblebody.com/blog/human-anatomy-atlas-virtual-3d-brain-dissection)

- Zygote — [3D Human Brain model](https://www.zygote.com/poly-models/3d-brain/3d-human-brain)

- [True-color 3D rendering from cadaver cryosection data (PMC)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9296043/)

---

## Visual acceptance checklist

Concrete, checkable criteria a reviewer uses to judge whether a rendered 3D brain "looks right." A pass requires **all** of the following:

**Overall form**
- [ ] Overall silhouette is an **ovoid, walnut-like mass that is broader at the back** and narrower at the front — **not** a smooth egg, peach, sphere, or featureless bean/blob.
- [ ] The surface is visibly **wrinkled/convoluted overall**, not smooth or glossy-plain.
- [ ] Proportions read as a brain, not a generic organ: cerebrum dominates (~80%+ of the mass), cerebellum is a much smaller posterior-inferior lump, brainstem is a thin stalk.

**Hemispheres and midline**
- [ ] **Two cerebral hemispheres are visibly separated by a deep midline groove** (the longitudinal fissure) running front-to-back along the top.
- [ ] The two hemispheres are **roughly mirror-image left/right halves**, joined only across the midline (not a single undivided dome).

**Cortical surface texture**
- [ ] The cortex shows distinct **gyri (raised ridges) and sulci (grooves)** — real folds, **not a smooth blob** and not a mere bumpy noise texture.
- [ ] Folds have **depth/shadowing in the sulci** (ambient occlusion), so the convolution pattern reads legibly under normal lighting rather than looking painted-on-flat.
- [ ] At least the major landmark sulci are plausibly present (a central sulcus running obliquely, a lateral/Sylvian fissure on the side); lobe regions are placeable if labelled.

**Cerebellum**
- [ ] A **distinct cerebellum is present at the posterior-inferior position**, tucked below and behind the occipital lobes.
- [ ] The cerebellum is **visually differentiated from the cerebrum** — smaller, with **fine, closely spaced parallel folia** rather than broad cerebral gyri (a "pleated"/finely-lined look).
- [ ] The cerebellum sits **behind the brainstem**, not fused seamlessly into the cerebrum.

**Brainstem and spinal cord**
- [ ] A **brainstem stalk projects downward from the underside/center**, between the cerebrum and cerebellum along the midline.
- [ ] The brainstem is **smoother than the cortex** and shows (or at least allows for) its three levels — midbrain, the bulging pons, tapering medulla.
- [ ] The brainstem **tapers and connects/continues into the spinal cord** at the base — not cut off abruptly, and not floating detached.

**Color and material**
- [ ] Overall tissue color is a warm **pinkish-grey / beige-tan** (living/fresh look) — **not** flat morgue-grey, and **not** unnatural saturated hues in the default naturalistic mode.
- [ ] White matter / brainstem read a **shade lighter and creamier** than the cortical grey-pink where exposed.
- [ ] Surface has a **soft moist sheen** (low-to-moderate gloss with a hint of translucency) — **not** dead-flat matte and **not** a plastic mirror finish.
- [ ] (If a cut plane is exposed) cross-section shows a **thin grey cortical rind over a larger paler white-matter core** — not uniform color throughout.

**Optional / mode-dependent**
- [ ] If a didactic mode exists, regions (cerebrum, cerebellum, brainstem, lobes) can be shown as **distinct flat colors** for identification.
- [ ] Meninges, if modelled, are **off by default** (bare cortex visible) and toggle on as separate translucent layers.

**Immediate fail conditions** (any one fails the review)
- [ ] Smooth, unfolded surface (egg/peach/sphere/bean).
- [ ] No visible midline separation between hemispheres.
- [ ] No cerebellum, or cerebellum indistinguishable from cerebrum.
- [ ] No brainstem, or brainstem not connecting toward the spinal cord.
- [ ] Wrong color identity (uniform flat grey with no pink/beige warmth, or arbitrary non-tissue colors in naturalistic mode).
